sub_claims.text: Wang et al. (2004), 'Image Quality Assessment: From Error Visibility to Structural Similarity' (IEEE TIP), introduces and defines the Structural Similarity (SSIM) index as a perceptual image quality measure built from luminance, contrast, and structure comparisons.

evidence.snippet: Under the assumption that human visual perception is highly adapted for extracting structural information from a scene, we introduce an alternative framework for quality assessment based on the degradation of structural information. As a specific example of this concept, we develop a Structural Similarity Index and demonstrate its promise through a set of intuitive examples, as well as comparison to both subjective ratings and state-of-the-art objective methods on a database of images compressed with JPEG and JPEG2000.

In Section III, we describe a new paradigm for quality assessment, based on the hypothesis that the HVS is highly adapted for extracting structural information. As a specific example, we develop a measure of structural similarity that compares local patterns of pixel intensities that have been normalized for luminance and contrast.

The system separates the task of similarity measurement into three comparisons: luminance, contrast and structure. First, the luminance of each signal is compared. Assuming discrete signals, this is estimated as the mean intensity...

Finally, we combine the three comparisons of Eqs. (6), (9) and (10) and name the resulting similarity measure the Structural SIMilarity (SSIM) index between signals x and y: ... where alpha > 0, beta > 0 and gamma > 0 are parameters used to adjust the relative importance of the three components.

It is easy to verify that this definition satisfies the three conditions given above. In order to simplify the expression, we set alpha = beta = gamma = 1 and C3 = C2/2 in this paper. This results in a specific form of the SSIM index...

sub_claims.nuance: The paper is universally recognized as the foundational SSIM reference. Section III.B is explicitly titled 'The Structural SIMilarity (SSIM) Index' and contains the canonical definition (luminance, contrast, structure comparisons combined into the SSIM formula). The abstract states 'we develop a Structural Similarity Index'. The Introduction (Section I) and the Framework section (III.A) describe the three comparisons (luminance, contrast, structure) that constitute SSIM. Note that the paper credits earlier conference papers [6]-[9] (Wang & Bovik) for an earlier 'universal quality index' (UQI) instantiation; this 2004 TIP paper generalizes that work and is the canonical journal-form SSIM definition cited in the literature.

phrasings_tried: we develop a Structural Similarity Index

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Section III.B and III.A note that an earlier instantiation of the structural-similarity approach appeared in Wang & Bovik refs [6]-[8] (the 'universal quality index' UQI; e.g., Wang & Bovik 2002 SPL), and that the present paper 'generalizes this algorithm.' However, the formal SSIM index, including the name 'Structural SIMilarity (SSIM) index' and the canonical luminance/contrast/structure formulation, is explicitly defined in this 2004 TIP paper. Citing zhouwang2004 as the source that introduces/defines SSIM is the standard and correct primary attribution.
attestation.out_of_context_check: No context mismatch. The paper's stated purpose is to introduce a new structural-similarity-based quality framework and to define the SSIM index as a specific instantiation; the manuscript claim mirrors that exactly.
